ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for flavoured packaged barley drink; closest match is the general barley beverage class. Flavoured and packaged specificity is not captured.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers barley-based beverages broadly; no specific code for flavoured packaged RTD barley drink exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for flavoured packaged barley drink; SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual commercial beverage products at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one commercially packaged can or bottle of approximately 300 ml (300 g), providing approximately 81 kcal and 20.1 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured barley drink (packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for flavoured barley drink (packaged), record ID D05010014. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened beverages and barley-based drinks — analogous estimate based on sucrose and glucose-containing beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for commercially packaged flavoured barley drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the following reasoning: the product contains 6.7 g/100 g of sugar (all carbohydrate is sugar, zero dietary fibre), making it functionally equivalent to a sugar-sweetened beverage. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values of 63–70 for sucrose-based sugar-sweetened beverages and 68–72 for glucose-containing soft drinks. A GI of 70 is applied as a conservative upper-bound analogous estimate. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (70 × 20.1 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 14.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage exceeding 5 g sugar per 100 g; independently associated with elevated blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this product as its 6.7 g sugar per 100 ml classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance, regardless of its very low sodium content of 4 mg per 100 ml; replace with unsweetened home-brewed barley water or plain water.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: All 6.7 g carbohydrate per 100 ml is sugar with zero dietary fibre, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this packaged barley drink because all carbohydrate (6.7 g/100 ml) is present as free sugar with no dietary fibre to moderate absorption, resulting in rapid blood glucose elevation; the estimated GI of 70 and glycemic load of 14.1 per 300 ml serving confirm high glycaemic impact, and the product should be replaced with unsweetened home-brewed barley water that retains beta-glucan fibre.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk via dyslipidaemia, insulin resistance, and visceral adiposity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this product because habitual sugar-sweetened beverage consumption (6.7 g sugar per 100 ml) is linked to elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased visceral fat — all established CVD risk factors — despite the product's negligible sodium and fat content; choose unsweetened beverages instead.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this product as its 6.7 g added sugar per 100 ml promotes inflammatory mediators while providing no anti-inflammatory nutrients (zero omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, or meaningful dietary fibre); replace with green tea, plain water, or unsweetened home-brewed barley water.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Barley Drink (Packaged) is a commercially produced, ready-to-drink (RTD) sweetened barley beverage widely available in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, typically sold in cans or bottles of 250–330 ml. It is made from water, barley extract, added sugar, and flavouring agents, and undergoes industrial processing that removes most of the naturally occurring beta-glucan dietary fibre found in whole barley grain. The drink is light, mildly sweet, and slightly grain-flavoured, with a thin, watery texture. It is commonly consumed as a refreshing beverage, particularly in warm weather, and is culturally associated with Chinese-Singaporean food traditions where barley water is a traditional cooling drink. Nutritionally, the packaged version is a low-calorie beverage at 27 kcal per 100 ml, but all 6.7 g of carbohydrate per 100 ml is present as free sugar with zero dietary fibre, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Protein and fat are both zero by product formulation. Sodium is very low at 4 mg per 100 ml. The estimated glycemic index is 70 (high), and a standard 300 ml serving delivers approximately 14.1 glycemic load units. Clinically, this product is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its SSB classification and high free-sugar content. It should not be confused with home-brewed unsweetened barley water, which retains beta-glucan fibre and has a different nutritional and glycaemic profile. The 'barley' label does not imply the fibre or cholesterol-lowering benefits of whole barley grain.
common_user_queries: Is barley drink good for diabetes?
Is barley drink suitable for hypertension?
Difference between packaged barley drink and home-brewed barley water
Is barley drink a sugar-sweetened beverage?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical primary source for Singapore foods) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food identity is unambiguous. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to a sugar-sweetened beverage with zero fibre, which is well-supported by published SSB GI data.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is confirmed zero by HPB source — this is a dilute barley-extract beverage with no protein-contributing ingredients by formulation. fat_g is confirmed zero by HPB source — no fat-containing ingredients are present in this product formulation. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — commercial processing removes beta-glucan fibre from barley extract. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source (confirmed zero). Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this packaged RTD barley drink are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D05010014, and no equivalent entry exists in USDA FoodData Central for this specific product. GI note: GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for sucrose-based sugar-sweetened beverages; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. Ontology note: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry exists for flavoured packaged barley drink. The product should not be confused with home-brewed unsweetened barley water (a distinct food with different nutritional properties). dietary_tags 'low_sodium' is technically accurate (4 mg/100 ml) but 'low_calorie' should be interpreted with caution as the product is a sugar-sweetened beverage.
